title: Federal Workers Can't Get the White House's App Off Their Phones
gist: The White House auto-pushed its propaganda app to the work phones of millions of federal employees across USDA, State Department, and DOL; employees cannot delete it—it reinstalls automatically. The app was built by a small Ohio WordPress shop (45Press, $1.5M contract) and incorporated widgets from a Russia-based company (Elfsight) that exposed White House officials' personal data. State Department employees are particularly worried because the app previously shared location and IP data with third parties and their phones are used to communicate with foreign contacts via WhatsApp.

themes.theme: Mandatory government propaganda app with security flaws

insights.claim: The White House auto-pushed its public-facing app to government-issued phones across at least USDA, State Department, and Department of Labor, and employees who deleted it found it automatically reinstalled—constituting a mandatory software deployment without employee consent.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Government MDM infrastructure being used to mandate political apps on federal devices is a governance and civil service integrity risk; raises questions about lawfulness of using federal IT systems for partisan communications.
insights.quote: I deleted it as a test and it came immediately back

insights.claim: The White House app was developed by 45Press, an Ohio-based company specializing in 'expert WordPress development' whose founder also runs a paranormal locations website directory—yet received $1.5 million in contracts and was eligible for over $8 million total, raising procurement fitness concerns.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Minimal-capability vendors receiving large federal app development contracts is a pattern of concern for government technology security; signals poor vetting standards in federal procurement.
insights.quote: According to its X account, specializes in 'expert WordPress development, design, hosting, ecommerce, and so much more.' According to the System for Award Management... 45Press received $1.5 million, and was eligible to receive more than $8 million, in government contracts.

insights.claim: An investigation found the White House app incorporated widgets from Russia-based company Elfsight, which exposed personal information of White House officials—a foreign software dependency discovered only through third-party investigation, not internal security review.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: A Russia-linked component in a mandatory federal government app is a significant supply chain security failure; underscores risks of using small underpowered vendors for government-mandated applications.
insights.quote: An investigation by NOTUS found that the app incorporated widgets created by a Russia-based company called Elfsight, which exposed the personal information of White House officials.

themes.theme: State Department foreign contacts security risk

insights.claim: State Department employees, who communicate with foreign contacts via WhatsApp on the same government phones now carrying the White House app, expressed concern that app vulnerabilities (including prior location/IP sharing with third parties) could compromise sensitive diplomatic communications.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: If sensitive foreign-contact communications are on the same device as a poorly-secured mandatory app, this creates a counterintelligence exposure; diplomatic security protocols may need to be revisited.
insights.quote: many of my foreign contacts use WhatsApp so we use that to communicate and I'm worried about the White House app vulnerabilities.

insights.claim: In April, cybersecurity experts flagged that the White House app initially shared users' locations and IP addresses with third parties—a vulnerability in an app now forcibly installed on State Department phones used for diplomatic work.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Location and IP data exfiltration from government devices is a counterintelligence risk; foreign adversaries could use this data to track the movements of federal employees globally.
insights.quote: in April, shortly after the app launched, cybersecurity experts flagged several concerns, including the fact that the app initially shared users' locations and IP addresses with third parties.

themes.theme: Federal app as political propaganda vehicle

insights.claim: The White House app's 'Social' section aggregates only Trump-favorable content from Fox, Breitbart, the New York Post, and White House press releases, with no opposing views, while its 'text President Trump' feature autofills the message 'Greatest President Ever'—functions that federal employees are required to carry on their work phones.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Mandatory carriage of overtly partisan propaganda on federal work devices may create Hatch Act or First Amendment exposure for the administration; a legal challenge could reach federal courts.
insights.quote: It's shooting pure unadulterated propaganda into our veins

themes.theme: Privacy policy opacity for mandatory government app

insights.claim: The White House app's App Store privacy policy page links to the WhiteHouse.gov privacy policy but provides only an email address for the mobile app itself—no data retention, sharing, or collection specifics—leaving federal employees with no information about how their app usage data is handled.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Absence of a mobile privacy policy for a mandatory government app could violate OMB/FISMA requirements and create legal exposure for the administration; watch for congressional oversight inquiries.
